bibliographic.citation: William Beamont, 'On Marriage Contracts, with Remarks on an Egerton Marriage Settlement, dated 10 Henry VI,' Journal of the Architectural, Archaeological, and Historic Society for the County, City, and Neighbourhood of Chester, old series 2 (1864): 183-202.
bibliographic.container_title: Journal of the Architectural, Archaeological, and Historic Society for the County, City, and Neighbourhood of Chester
bibliographic.publisher: Architectural, Archaeological, and Historic Society for the County, City, and Neighbourhood of Chester
bibliographic.title: On Marriage Contracts, with Remarks on an Egerton Marriage Settlement, dated 10 Henry VI

entities.description: A twenty-page antiquarian article surveying marriage contracts and discussing an Egerton settlement. Beamont prints a full-length transcription in an extended footnote on pp. 197-199 and a reduced photographic plate between pp. 196 and 197. His main text argues that the dated settlement is spurious and possibly a contemporary forgery; that analysis is outside CME00127.
entities.label: William Beamont, On Marriage Contracts (1864)
notes: CME00127 is not a complete encoding of this article. It selects the p. 183 subtitle and the footnote headnote/transcription zones on pp. 197-199.

rights.basis: The article and plate were published in 1864 and are outside copyright term; provider-specific access terms for modern scan services remain separate.
rights.component: Beamont's 1864 article, transcription, editorial prose, pedigree, and published plate

access.accessMethod: No publicly cataloged complete physical-object facsimile was located; only Beamont's one-quarter-size view of one surface is known
access.entityLabel: Original Egerton marriage-settlement indenture seen by Beamont
notes: This is a dated negative search result, not a claim that no complete facsimile exists or can be obtained.
A complete surrogate would need every surviving side and physical surface, including reverse and seal area, with an identified current holder.

access.accessMethod: Ask Cheshire Archives whether the deed survives in an uncataloged or differently described Oulton-family series, and for advice on any later transfer
access.entityLabel: Original Egerton marriage-settlement indenture seen by Beamont
notes: DEO is the current Egerton of Oulton estate collection, but its public description begins in the sixteenth century and no exact record for this document was identified. This route does not assert that Cheshire Archives holds the original.
Cheshire Archives states that collection access is on hold until its new centers open in early 2027; enquiries remain the appropriate next step.

dateStatements.label: Date stated by the document and interpreted by Beamont
dateStatements.notes: The formula and Beamont's calendar conversion are direct evidence for the date claimed by the document, not proof of its actual execution date; Beamont disputes its authenticity.
dateStatements.value: Feast of St Cuthbert, 10 Henry VI (20 March 1432)

dateStatements.label: Item-specific historical possession
dateStatements.notes: Beamont explicitly says the original was then in Sir Philip Grey Egerton's possession.

entities.description: The physical document that Beamont said was in Sir Philip Grey Egerton's possession in 1864. Beamont describes it as an indenture dated by its text to the Feast of St Cuthbert in 10 Henry VI, interprets that date as 20 March 1432, reports one former seal position but no surviving seal, and identifies it as Ralph Egerton's part. Its present location, shelfmark, support, reverse, and condition have not been established.
entities.label: Original Egerton marriage-settlement indenture seen by Beamont
notes: Sir Philip Grey Egerton's 1864 possession is historical provenance, not a current holding.
Beamont's description of the settlement as spurious and likely a contemporary forgery is preserved as an attributed scholarly judgment.

physicalDescription.extent: One long indenture document; one visible surface reproduced in the 1864 plate
physicalDescription.format: Indenture or deed, according to Beamont
notes: Beamont says no word was bisected in forming the indenture's top edge.
Beamont reports one former seal position but says no seal remained.
The reduced plate is insufficient to establish support, dimensions, reverse contents, complete physical coverage, color, or present condition.

rights.basis: The anonymous historical text was published in full in 1864 and any copyright term has expired; this does not resolve ownership or access to the physical object or rights in later images and markup.
rights.component: The Middle English settlement text, whether created in the fifteenth century or no later than its 1864 publication

entities.description: A rotated photographic illustration published between printed pp. 196 and 197. reviewNotes: The primary subject is the repository artifact CME/source/CME_phase_3/CME00127.xml. The U-M encoding, 1864 article, original document, published photographic plate, and modern digital scans are separate entities.
The XML HEAD is the article subtitle printed on p. 183, not a heading visible on p. 197. Its placement after PB N=197 is an encoding arrangement and does not establish a p. 197 source locus.

The encoded prose is spatially zoned rather than page-complete. On printed pp. 197-199 Beamont's main analysis continues above or alongside the footnote transcription; CME00127 retains only the footnote headnote and quoted document text.

On printed p. 199 the encoded transcription occupies a separate mid-page block and ends with 'Yeven the yere and day abovesaide' before the pedigree. Beamont's spurious-settlement conclusion in the surrounding main text is not encoded.

Beamont describes the document as an indenture, dates its formula to 20 March 1432, notes one former seal position but no surviving seal, and argues that it was a contemporary forgery. These are attributed 1864 judgments, not silently adopted physical or authenticity facts.

The published photographic plate appears to reproduce one long document surface in a single reduced view. It does not show a reverse or establish complete physical-object coverage, preservation-master resolution, color fidelity, or the object's present condition.

The last item-specific holding statement found is Beamont's 1864 report that the original belonged to Sir Philip Grey Egerton. Cheshire Archives' current Egerton of Oulton collection DEO is contextual only; no exact current catalog record for this settlement was found on 2026-07-13.

The repository root license at the pinned CME revision is the software-worded Unlicense. Its application to corpus XML, markup, and data is not explicit, so the repository-artifact rights status remains unknown rather than being broadened from the underlying public-domain text and edition.

lineage.summary: The checked-in XML is a repository representation of University of Michigan CME00127; matching identifiers and content establish version identity, but the exact import or copy direction is undocumented. Its immediate source is a discontiguous selection from William Beamont's 1864 article: the article subtitle on printed p. 183 is relocated as the XML HEAD, and Beamont's editorial headnote plus his full-length transcription occupy separate footnote zones on printed pp. 197-199. The encoding omits the surrounding article analysis, including Beamont's argument that the settlement was spurious. Beamont says he transcribed an original then in Sir Philip Grey Egerton's possession and published a one-quarter-size photographic view of one visible document surface. The original's current holding is unknown. A complete 22-page ADS article PDF and a complete 653-page Internet Archive volume scan, OCR, scan metadata, and item metadata are cached locally with fixity; none is a complete physical-object facsimile of the original deed.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous
catalogSummary.date: Dated 20 March 1432 (authenticity disputed); first known publication 1864
catalogSummary.region: Cheshire, England (documentary associations); scribe possibly Cheshire or the Welsh border
catalogSummary.title: An Egerton Marriage Settlement, dated 10 Henry VI

catalogingSubject.label: An Egerton Marriage Settlement, dated 10 Henry VI
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is the anonymous Middle English document arranging the marriage of William Egerton and Margerie Egerton and associated property, dower, payment, and inheritance provisions. The document calls itself an indenture and bears a date formula equivalent to 20 March 1432, but William Beamont's surrounding 1864 analysis argues that it is spurious and probably a contemporary forgery; actual creation date and authenticity therefore remain unresolved. It is not Beamont's complete article, the article's authenticity argument, the physical document as an object, the 1864 plate, the U-M encoding, or the checked-in XML artifact. CME00127 retains Beamont's supplied article subtitle and editorial headnote as distinct paratext.

subtitle PDF p. 1; footnote headnote PDF p. 17
HEAD and HEADNOTE/P after PB N=197

notes: The HEAD's XML position after PB N=197 is not evidence that the subtitle originated on printed p. 197.

contentParts.label: Middle English marriage-settlement text

locators.value: document text on the original in Sir Philip Grey Egerton's possession in 1864; current shelfmark unknown
footnote transcription zones on pp. 197-199; p. 199 endpoint before the pedigree
PDF pp. 17-19, excluding Beamont's continuing main-text analysis on the same printed pages
DIV1/P from 'Thes endentures' through 'Yeven the yere and day abovesaide'; PB N=197-199

notes: Beamont's surrounding argument that the settlement is spurious belongs to article commentary, not this content part.
contentParts.summary: The full quoted settlement from 'Thes endentures' through 'Yeven the yere and day abovesaide.'

assertion.notes: The text directly supplies the regnal and feast-day formula, and Beamont converts it to 20 March 1432. Because his surrounding analysis argues that the instrument is spurious, the normalized date is a claimed execution date rather than a confirmed creation date.

dateStatements.display: Dated 20 March 1432 (the Feast of St Cuthbert in 10 Henry VI); authenticity disputed

assertion.notes: Beamont's publication is the earliest item-specific attestation verified in this review; this is not a claim that no earlier reference exists.

This is the first publication identified in the sources reviewed, not an exhaustive proof that no earlier publication exists. Beamont presents the settlement as an original then in private possession and prints it at length.

extent.description: One prose settlement in one DIV1 and one P, preceded by one prose editorial headnote in one P and a supplied HEAD. The body also contains three PB milestones, two italic '(sic)' markers, and four SUP elements. The p. 183 heading and pp. 197-199 prose are discontiguous source loci despite their continuous XML arrangement.

formStatements.description: A prose legal instrument framed by a brief prose editorial headnote in the encoded item.

assertion.notes: Genre describes the instrument's textual form and professed function without deciding its legal authenticity.
The instrument uses indenture, covenant, feoffment, remainder, obligation, warranty, and sealing formulas.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: Modern English in Beamont's supplied title and headnote is editorial paratext and is not promoted to a work-level language facet.
The preferred title is an 1864 supplied access title, not a demonstrated native heading on the original document.
The XML's PB N=197 precedes a HEAD copied from printed p. 183; page-break order therefore cannot be used as a literal source-page provenance claim.
Beamont's main analysis is interleaved with the footnote transcription on printed pp. 197-199. The XML includes only the footnote headnote and document text, ending mid-page before the pedigree.
The form and genre statements describe the document's textual and professed legal character without deciding its authenticity.
Modern English editorial framing is described at part level and does not make the cataloged historical work bilingual.
Sir Philip Grey Egerton's possession is securely stated for 1864 only; current holding and complete physical-object image coverage remain unknown.

next_steps: Locate later editions, documentary studies, or a modern item-level catalog that evaluates Beamont's conclusion.
If the original is found, compare script, language, support, ink, indenture edge, seal area, arithmetic, parties, and land provisions with securely dated Egerton deeds.
Until then, retain 20 March 1432 as the document's claimed date and Beamont's forgery conclusion as an attributed interpretation.
openQuestions.question: What actual creation date and authenticity status should current scholarship assign to the settlement?

next_steps: Ask Cheshire Archives about uncataloged or differently described Oulton-family deeds without assuming that collection DEO contains the item.
Trace Egerton family and Oulton Hall estate records after Sir Philip Grey Egerton's 1864 possession.
Request every surviving physical surface once the holder is identified; keep the 1864 plate as one-surface partial coverage.
openQuestions.question: Where is the original document now, and can an exact current shelfmark, provenance chain, and complete reproduction be established?

assertion.notes: The named Egerton parties and principal property references belong to Cheshire contexts. This association does not by itself prove where or when the surviving document was made.
The instrument provides for lands and tenements in Chester and other places.
The text names Wich Malbank, the historical name of Nantwich, as a location of settled lands.
Beamont says the spelling suggests a Cheshire scribe or a Welsh borderer. Cheshire is retained as one attributed possibility, not a confirmed place of copying or composition.
Responsibility is anonymous at the textual-document layer; named covenanting parties are not converted into authors without evidence of drafting or inscription.
Beamont says the appended text is a copy of the settlement at length from the original then in Sir Philip Grey Egerton's possession.
The preferred title and headnote belong to Beamont's nineteenth-century editorial layer, not to the anonymous document.

summaries.value: A Middle English indenture-form document arranging the intended marriage of William, son and heir of Philip Egerton, to Margerie, daughter of Ralph Egerton of the Wryne. It allocates clothing costs, directs feoffments and remainders in lands at Wich Malbank, Chester, and elsewhere, protects Alice Egerton's dower, specifies marriage payments and installments, imposes financial penalties for breach, and ends with a sealing formula. The document bears a date equivalent to 20 March 1432, but its authenticity and actual creation date are disputed.

CME00127 is a discontiguous selection from Beamont's article. It relocates the article subtitle printed on p. 183 as the XML HEAD, then encodes the p. 197 footnote headnote and the quoted settlement in separate footnote zones on pp. 197-199. Beamont's main analysis continues on those same pages, including the spurious-settlement conclusion at the top of p. 199, but is excluded; the encoding ends within p. 199 before the pedigree and does not include the photographic plate.

Beamont converts the document's date formula to 20 March 1432 but argues from genealogical and inquisition evidence that the settlement is spurious, while proposing that it may be a contemporary forgery connected with an actual later marriage between the named parties. This remains an attributed 1864 interpretation: the manifest neither validates the deed as an authentic 1432 legal instrument nor promotes Beamont's conclusion to settled modern consensus.

assertion.notes: This established access title is Beamont's printed article subtitle on p. 183. It is not demonstrated to be a title written on the original document, and the encoding relocates it after PB N=197.

titles.supplied_by: William Beamont in the 1864 source article
